role_summary: Founding chairman of the Department of Afro-American Studies at UMass Amherst (1970); director of SNCC's Washington office (1963); novelist of The Harder They Come (1980); editor of Stokely Carmichael's memoir Ready for Revolution (2003).
biographical_paragraph: In 1970, at thirty-one, Ekwueme Michael Thelwell became the founding chairman of the Department of Afro-American Studies at the University of Massachusetts at Amherst [src: 2]. He had come to the post through SNCC's Washington office, which he directed from 1963 [src: 2]. Born July 25, 1939 in Ulster Spring, Jamaica, Thelwell took a Bachelor's at Howard University in 1964 and an MFA from UMass Amherst in 1969 [src: 2]. He is the author of the 1980 novel The Harder They Come, built from the Perry Henzell film about the Jamaican folk hero Ivanhoe "Rhyging" Martin; the book drew attention from Chinua Achebe, and Harold Bloom placed it in the appendix to his The Western Canon [src: 2]. In 2003 Thelwell prepared and edited the political memoir of his SNCC colleague Stokely Carmichael, Ready for Revolution: The Life and Struggles of Stokely Carmichael [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 34 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Stokely Carmichael with Ekwueme Michael Thelwell, Ready for Revolution: The Life and Struggles of Stokely Carmichael (Kwame Ture)
